narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to raise concerns for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings of 4—below the typical student's 5.71 and 5.53 averages—along with your LSAT score of 25, well under the usual 36.7, a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 just above the 3.21 average, middle family income bracket, Asian background, female gender, and plan to study full-time. Compared to most applicants, who tend to have stronger early rankings, higher LSATs, and often come from White backgrounds in higher income groups, your profile showed some hurdles that the model weighed heavily. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as it signaled potential challenges in sustaining performance. Right behind that were your Year 1 decile and low LSAT score, both contributing noticeably to the concern about bar passage. Your middle family income and Asian background followed closely in influence, also nudging the model toward expecting difficulty. Even your slightly above-average undergraduate GPA played a supporting role in that direction, though less strongly. On a brighter note, being female and planning full-time study slightly favored a passing prediction, countering the negatives just a bit. Overall, your Year 3 performance edged out as the top influence, with LSAT a close second, highlighting where gaps stood out most against peers. While some elements like past grades, LSAT, family income, race, and gender are harder to change now, you could focus on bolstering bar exam preparation through targeted practice, study groups, or courses to build on your solid GPA and full-time commitment. Retaking the LSAT for a stronger score, if feasible, might help future applications by addressing that key weakness. With your strengths in undergraduate performance and dedication, proactive steps like these could shift the odds in your favor for passing the bar and succeeding in law school. Keep pushing—you have a good foundation to build from.
